rubric_en: Humankind has devised a new form of debasement.
rubric_zh: 人类已经发明了新的堕落方式。

paragraphs.en: Sometimes, staring brainlessly into my laptop in the trough of a weekday afternoon, experiencing myself as a kind of online shadow, a thing of fidgets, a half-being hollowed out by roaming spectral appetites—for destruction, for gratification, for the email that never comes—it occurs to me to ask: Now, which of the seven deadly sins is this?
paragraphs.zh: 有时，在一个平日午后的下半场里，我呆呆地盯着笔记本电脑，自己像一个网络中的影子，像一团不停扭动的杂念，一个半个人半空壳，被徘徊不定的幽灵般欲望——为了毁灭、为了满足、为了那封始终不来邮件——所掏空。于是我会想：这到底对应哪一种七宗原罪？

paragraphs.en: Anger’s in there somewhere, sure, a kind of generalized psychic road rage, but not enough of it to qualify. Same with envy, pride, gluttony, lust—just floating shards. Avarice? Nah. Of the seven, sloth probably comes closest, that enigmatic void state known to the early Christians as “acedia.” But not even acedia, bottomless as it is, can quite comprehend this plugged-in groundlessness, this ether-sweeping emptiness, this interstellar elongation of the spirit. Sin, the theologians tell us, is whatever separates us from God. Whatever blocks the beams of divine love. And at 3:23 p.m. in Caffè Nero, I am all but unreachable by heavenly radiation; I can feel it wavering, honey-colored, at the fringes of my soul. So have we done it at last—you, me, the kids? Have we invented an eighth deadly sin?
paragraphs.zh: 愤怒当然在其中，像某种泛化的精神“路怒”，但远远不足以构成这一类。嫉妒、骄傲、暴食、色欲也一样，只是零散的碎片。贪婪？不。七宗原罪里，懒惰大概最接近这一点，它在早期基督徒那里叫做“灵性厌倦”（acedia）。可就算是无底的灵性厌倦，也还不足以包容这种插上电源的失足感，这种像以太般扫荡一切的空虚，这种将灵魂拉长到星际的状态。神学家告诉我们，原罪是让我们与上帝分离的任何东西，是一切阻隔神圣爱光线的东西。于是，在伦敦 Caffè Nero 的下午3点23分，我几乎与天辐射失去联系；我能感觉到它在我灵魂边缘微微摇曳，像蜂蜜色的光。那我们终于做到了吗——你我、孩子们？我们是不是已经发明了第八宗大罪？

paragraphs.en: I thought initially that the title of Peter Jones’s Self-Help From the Middle Ages: What the Seven Deadly Sins Can Teach Us About Living was an oxymoron. Self-help is our thing, after all, our exemplary piece of circular modernity, our little closed circuit—the distressed subject coming to its own aid. The medievals, more vertical in their thinking, would have counted on the down-rushing swoop of God’s grace.
paragraphs.zh: 起初我觉得彼得·琼斯的《中世纪自助：七宗大罪告诉我们如何生活》这个书名本身就是个悖论。自助毕竟是我们这一代人的事，我们那个光彩夺目的循环现代性样本，我们的“小型闭环”——陷入困境的人来救助自己。中世纪的人思维更“垂直”，他们会把希望寄托在上帝恩典的下倾俯冲上。

paragraphs.en: But Jones, a medieval historian, shows us that the High and Later Middle Ages (1100 to 1500, roughly) were every bit as goofy as we are about human nature and behavior, and equally hooked on buzzwords, listicles (Catherine of Siena’s five different types of tears, Thomas Aquinas’s five varieties of gluttony), and junk science. To explain the inexplicable (that is, themselves), the medievals used the 12 signs of the zodiac, the four humors—yellow bile, black bile, blood, and phlegm—and the seven deadly sins. Jones takes the seven one by one, a chapter for each, arguing that this gnarly old taxonomy represents not only a timeless decoction of human wisdom but something of a moral map for our present wanderings. I think I agree with him, but let’s see.
paragraphs.zh: 但琼斯这位中世纪史家告诉我们，高中世纪和后中世纪（大约1100年至1500年）的人类本性和行为认知一点也不比我们轻松，不亚于我们一样热衷流行词汇、清单文章（包括圣塞巴斯蒂安的五种眼泪、托马斯·阿奎那的五类暴食）和伪科学。为了去解释难以解释的东西（也就是他们自己），中世纪的人用了十二生肖、四种体液——黄胆汁、黑胆汁、血液和痰——以及七宗大罪。琼斯逐一展开这七罪，每一种都单独成章，并主张这种粗粝而古老的分类，不仅是超时代的人类智慧萃取，也像一张道德地图，指向我们当下的漂泊。我想我同意他的观点，但我们先看一看。

paragraphs.en: It turns out that there were eight, at least in the beginning. Evagrius Ponticus was one of the world-abandoning superstar Desert Fathers of fourth-century Egypt. Influencer-like, he stood in freezing wells and subsisted on bread and oil, and after much torment and cogitation, he drew up a list of what he called “generic thoughts,” or routine invaders of the spirit: gluttony, lust, avarice, sadness, anger, sloth, vainglory, and pride. Subsequent spiritual engineers made their tweaks to Evagrius’s system, but the decisive overhaul was performed 200 years later, by Pope Gregory the Great. He rolled sadness into sloth, vainglory into pride, added envy, and voilà: the seven deadly sins.
paragraphs.zh: 起初，至少有八条。4 世纪埃及的世界弃世级沙漠教父厄瓦格留斯·庞蒂库斯（Evagrius Ponticus）就是其中一位。这个像“网红”一样的人常年站在冰冷的井边，以面包和橄榄油为食。经过长期的苦修与沉思，他列出了一份他称之为“通用思想”或“常规侵扰精神的念头”的清单：贪食、色欲、贪婪、忧愁、愤怒、懒惰、虚荣和骄傲。后来的一些精神导师在厄瓦格留斯体系上做了修订，但真正决定性的大修是在 200 年后由伟大教皇格里高利完成的。他把忧愁并入懒惰，把虚荣并入骄傲，又加入了嫉妒，于是就有了“七宗致命罪”。

paragraphs.en: From the November 2024 issue: Hillary Kelly on Oliver Burkeman’s unlikely approach to self-help
paragraphs.zh: ——摘自 2024 年 11 月期：希拉里·凯利（Hillary Kelly）写下的《奥利弗·伯克曼（Oliver Burkeman）不走寻常路的自助方式》

paragraphs.en: Now the sins were characters. They could be personified, and artists went to town. An illustration for Guillaume de Deguileville’s 14th-century poem “The Pilgrimage of Human Life” depicts avarice as a woman whose arms have been cut off (she retains the stumps) and replaced with allegorical limbs—feathered claws for rapaciousness, hands holding begging bowls and scales for moneylending, and so on. And is her tongue hanging out? Is she drooling with money-lust? It looks like it. Giotto painted envy as a woman—yes, the medievals, like us, were misogynists—a slanderous monster with a snake squirming out of her mouth, turning back, and reentering her head at the eyes. Also, she has huge, swiveling, batlike ears. And her feet are on fire.
paragraphs.zh: 后来，这些罪就有了“角色”属性。它们可以被人格化，于是艺术家纷纷发挥想象力。纪尧姆·德·居列维勒（Guillaume de Deguileville）14 世纪诗作《人的一生朝圣路途》中的一幅插图，将贪婪描绘成一位手臂被切断却仍留有残肢的女人，并改换成象征性的肢体：用羽毛爪代表贪得无厌的掠夺欲、手中端着施舍钵和天平象征放贷借贷，诸如此类。她的舌头是不是还伸着？是不是在流着对金钱的贪馋口水？看起来像是。吉奥托（Giotto）则把嫉妒画作一位女性——是的，中世纪的人和我们一样，也是反对女性的——一个诽谤性的怪物，她口中有条蛇蜿蜒出来，再转身从眼睛处钻回她的头部。而且她还长着巨大的、可旋转的、像蝙蝠一样的耳朵。她的脚正在着火。

paragraphs.en: Ghastly as they are, these allegorical images don’t really touch us moderns, or not deeply. Much more disturbing and relatable, in Giotto’s case, are the sour-faced onlookers and eavesdroppers who lurk at the edges of his frescoes in the Arena Chapel, in Padua: the servant watching in disaffection as an angel tells Anne that she is going to give birth to the Virgin Mary; the scowling, black-veiled woman overhearing Anne tell her husband, Joachim, that she has conceived. To be one of these characters, Jones writes, is to be a “bitter witness.” We know these people. We are these people. Social media: a cloud of bitter witnesses! This is envy—invidia—as it lives inside us, shriveling our spirits and kindling our meannesses.
paragraphs.zh: 这些寓言式图像再恐怖，也只是让我们现代人“有点刺痛”，却不至于深深触动。相比之下，更令人不安、也更贴近现实的，在吉奥托的作品里，是位于帕多瓦竞技场小堂（Arena Chapel）壁画边缘那些阴沉的观者和窃听者：仆人在一边不满地看着天使告诉安妮（Anne）她将诞下圣母玛利亚；那位皱着眉的黑纱女士则偷听安妮向丈夫乔阿基姆（Joachim）透露她已经怀孕。琼斯写道，成为这类人物，就是成为一个“苦涩见证者（bitter witness）”。我们认识这些人。我们就是这些人。社交媒体：一团团充满苦味的见证者！这就是嫉妒——invidia——它活在我们体内，让我们精神萎缩，点燃我们的卑劣与阴暗。

paragraphs.en: Because this is 2026 and everything’s got to be personal, Self-Help From the Middle Ages prefaces each chapter, each sin, with a reminiscence from the author’s time teaching medieval history at a university in Siberia. Hemmed in by the cold, under laboratory conditions as it were, Jones self-examines in the lurid light of the seven deadlies. His transgressions are meek—a bit of ogling at the hot springs (lust), a snippy comment in a faculty meeting (anger). He doesn’t trash the common room in an alcoholic blaze or crucify a colleague’s cat. But this is precisely the point: The sins are quotidian, undramatic, regular human business. You can do all seven without leaving your house. The trick is to recognize the sins, to “name” them (in a modern but very appropriate locution). They offer a kind of diagnostic prism, refracting the black, primordial beam of sin into lesser rays of the identifiably and manageably human. And once you know what you’re dealing with, you can summon its opposite. If pride is getting the better of you, engage humility; if gluttony, moderation; if envy, compassion; and so on.
paragraphs.zh: 因为这是2026年，而且一切都要强调个人体验，《中世纪自助》一书在每一章、每一宗罪前都以作者在西伯利亚某大学讲授中世纪历史时的往事作开场。身处严寒之中，在某种“实验室条件”里，琼斯以七宗罪的艳丽光芒审视自己。他的过失并不激烈——在温泉边多看了一眼（欲望），在一次系务会议上说了一句刻薄的话（愤怒）。他并不会在酗酒冲动下闹翻休息室，也不会把同事的猫活活钉死。可这正是重点：这些罪并不戏剧化，而是日常的、平常的、普通人的日常事务。你不出门就能犯下全部七宗罪。关键在于识别它们，并“命名”它们（这是一个很现代，也很恰当的说法）。它们像一种诊断棱镜，把罪恶那道黑色、原初的光束折射成更小、可辨识且可管理的人性化光线。只要你明白自己在对付什么，就可以请出它的对立面。若骄傲占了上风，就以谦卑应对；若贪食，就用克制；若嫉妒，就用同情，诸如此类。

paragraphs.en: Otherwise they’ll hold us, they’ll dominate us, these sins. While I was reading the chapter on anger, I experienced a surge in understanding as to the nature of sin itself. Sin is subtle, seductive, addictive, of course: The medical encyclopedia The Property of Things (circa 1240) describes anger very perceptively as a “wave of bliss.” (Oh, the white-hot elation of righteousness, carrying me away!) But it’s also rigid and carceral, a system of control. Jones gives us the 13th-century Catalonian doctor Arnaud de Vilanova, who, after limited success treating anger with the miracle drug theriac (made from viper’s flesh sweetened with honey, a distillation of lilies, and about 40 other ingredients), fell to pondering the character of the malady itself. As Jones writes, “Fury pulls the mind away from reason (ratio), Arnaud reflected, and losing yourself in anger is like letting a puppeteer take control of your brain as well as your limbs.”
paragraphs.zh: 否则它们会束缚我们，会支配我们，这些罪。在读到愤怒这一章时，我对罪的本质有了更深一层的理解。罪当然是微妙的、诱人的、令人上瘾的，医学百科全书《The Property of Things》（约1240年）非常准确地把愤怒描述为“狂喜的浪潮”。（啊，那种正义的炽热欣快感，会把人卷走！）但它同样是僵硬而又监禁般的，是一种控制系统。琼斯介绍了13世纪的加泰罗尼亚医生阿诺德·德·维拉诺瓦，他在用一种神奇药物“解毒剂”治疗愤怒时只取得有限成功；那种“解毒剂”由毒蛇肉加蜂蜜、百合蒸馏液等约40种成分制成。于是他开始思考病症本身。正如琼斯写道：“阿诺德反思道，狂怒会把理性（ratio）夺走，沉溺于愤怒之中就像让一位木偶师同时操控你的大脑和四肢。”

paragraphs.en: A puppeteer controlling your brain? Heavy-metal fans know where this is going—straight to the primary text of compulsion and merciless soul-negation, Metallica’s “Master of Puppets”: “Master of puppets, I’m pulling your strings / Twisting your mind and smashing your dreams / Blinded by me, you can’t see a thing / Just call my name ’cause I’ll hear you scream.” This is where the personification of the seven deadlies comes in handy: You, you naughty person, you libertine, rage beast, sybarite, whatever, might think that you’re committing a sin. But it’s the sin, the master of puppets, that is committing you. Unless you can turn, look it in the face, and summon the right ally.
paragraphs.zh: 木偶师控制你的大脑？重金属爱好者都知道这会通向哪里——直接通向那首关于强迫与冷酷灵魂否认的根文本，金属乐队 Metallica 的《Master of Puppets》：“木偶师之主，我在牵着你的丝线 / 扭曲你的思想，粉碎你的梦想 / 被我蒙蔽，你什么也看不见 / 只要喊我的名字，因为我会听到你的尖叫。” 在这里，给七宗罪拟人是很有帮助的：你这位不守规矩的人、你这位放纵者、暴怒野兽、逸乐者……也许会以为是自己在犯一种罪。其实是罪——这位木偶大师——在“犯下”你。除非你能转身、直视它，并请出合适的对手。

paragraphs.en: As for the eighth sin, the new one—having Wi-Fi—is there anything to be done about that? Naturally, excessive online-ness has its counter-impulses: the digital detoxes and the dopamine fasts, all in the fine old spirit of medieval asceticism. You can go online right now and learn about how to be offline. But to free ourselves from this one, to even have the beginnings of a program to free ourselves, I think we’re going to have to do the truly and terrifyingly medieval thing. We’re going to have to get on our knees and pray.
paragraphs.zh: 至于第八宗罪，也就是新的那种——拥有 Wi-Fi——我们能做些什么？当然，过度“在线”也有它的反制手段：数字排毒和戒多巴胺般的“禁玩”，都带着中古禁欲传统那种古旧却精妙的精神。你现在上网就能找到如何“离线”的方法。但要想从这一点中解放出来，要有一套真正开始解放的方案，我想我们得做一件真正可怕而中世纪式的事：我们得跪下祈祷。

paragraphs.en: This article appears in the May 2026 print edition with the headline “The Eighth Deadly Sin.”
paragraphs.zh: 本文刊登于2026年5月的印刷版，标题为“第八宗大罪”。
